current_fact.fact_text: On March 5, 1967, Lake Central Airlines Flight 527 crashed near Marseilles, Ohio, after a propeller blade snapped off in flight and tore into the fuselage. The failure led to changes in how propeller fatigue was monitored, making skies safer for later travelers.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

current_fact.match_reason: Aviation safety built on painful lessons aligns with an episode about discipline, training, and how repeated practice turns risk into controlled movement.

queue.fact_text: On March 5, 1973, an Iberia DC-9 and a Spantax Convair 990 collided in mid-air above Nantes, France, killing all 68 people aboard the DC‑9, including Jimi Hendrix’s former manager Michael Jeffery. The tragedy showed how invisible, intersecting flight paths can change musical history as much as any contract.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: A behind-the-scenes music figure lost in an air disaster fits a foundational episode that honours the web of artists and stories behind a musical life.

queue.fact_text: On March 5, 1970, the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons entered into force, as 43 nations agreed to limit the spread of nuclear arms. The treaty turned the ultimate weapon into a shared global anxiety and an ongoing diplomatic balancing act.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: A treaty aimed at preventing ultimate catastrophe aligns with a hopeful, weather-themed episode about storms passing and collective optimism.

queue.fact_text: On March 5, 2002, a magnitude 7.5 earthquake struck off Mindanao in the southern Philippines, triggering landslides and collapsing structures. Fifteen people were killed and more than a hundred injured, a stark reminder of how quickly tectonic forces can reshape daily life.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: A tectonic jolt under the sea connects with an episode about the hidden, powerful world beneath the ocean’s surface and our fragile place within it.

queue.fact_text: In 2012, Tropical Storm Irina looped around the southwest Indian Ocean and hammered Madagascar and nearby coasts, killing over 75 people. Though never a powerful hurricane, its long, erratic path caused widespread flooding, landslides, and coastal erosion.

episode.title: Morning Drops #23 - Morning Dubs
episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.
songs: The Gaylads - Joy in the Morning
King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown

queue.match_reason: A looping tropical storm impacting African coasts pairs with a rootsy, Caribbean-influenced dub selection often associated with island climates, rain, and resilience.

queue.fact_text: On March 5, 2018, in the complex mosaic of the Syrian civil war, the Kurdish-led Syrian Democratic Forces paused their Deir ez-Zor offensive to respond to Turkey’s invasion of Afrin. In a conflict already full of shifting fronts, it was a moment when one battle literally had to stop to make room for another.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: An episode about music born from desert conflict and displacement resonates with a modern war story shaped by shifting alliances, land, and survival.

queue.fact_text: On March 5, 2002, the same day as the Mindanao quake, scientists continued to refine global seismic networks that now detect thousands of earthquakes a day—most too small to feel. Events like Mindanao underscore why seismology quietly listens to Earth’s constant, rumbling heartbeat.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: The idea of listening in on Earth’s low, constant tremors matches a genre built on subtle pulses, ambience, and layers that reward deep listening.

queue.fact_text: By March 5, 2012, Madagascar had already endured years of intense cyclones and storms like Irina, making it a hotspot for studying how climate change may be amplifying extreme weather. Its unique ecosystems are forced to adapt not just to human activity, but to increasingly unpredictable skies.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

queue.match_reason: An island whose seasons are increasingly shaped by storms is paired with an episode meditating on long summers and changing climates, through fluid, weather-like jazz.

queue.fact_text: On March 5, 1963, the day Patsy Cline’s plane went down, she had already recorded "Crazy," a song that would echo through decades of covers and playlists. Her death froze her catalogue in time, turning a relatively brief career into something mythic.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

queue.match_reason: The story of a legendary female voice lost too soon fits an episode celebrating the enduring power of women’s voices in music.

queue.fact_text: By March 5, 2018, the Syrian civil war had become a patchwork of front lines, with local communities caught between airstrikes, shifting alliances, and frozen campaigns like the SDF’s pause in Deir ez-Zor. For many civilians, "pause" didn’t mean peace—just a brief reconfiguration of danger.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

queue.match_reason: A complex, politically charged conflict with human cost pairs with an episode centered on a fiercely political artist whose work confronts power, identity, and war.

published.fact_text: In 1979, three different spacecraft—Soviet probes Venera 11 and 12, and the German-American solar satellite Helios II—were simultaneously blasted by “off the scale” gamma rays. This mysterious burst led astronomers to identify a new kind of cosmic object: soft gamma repeaters, neutron stars with magnetic fields so strong they can distort atoms themselves.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A story about extreme cosmic phenomena pairs with an episode themed around "space" and shifting perceptions of emptiness into possibility.

published.fact_text: On March 5, 1982, the Soviet probe Venera 14 survived a fiery plunge through the Venusian atmosphere and sent back color images from one of the harshest surfaces in the Solar System. It operated for just 57 minutes before being silenced by temperatures hot enough to melt lead and crushing atmospheric pressure about 90 times that of Earth.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: Venera 14’s descent through Venus’s dense atmosphere mirrors the episode’s slow, atmospheric build that eventually opens into wider, warmer landscapes.

published.fact_text: Landsat 3, launched on March 5, 1978, continued a quiet revolution: taking systematic photographs of Earth from space so scientists could watch deserts expand, forests vanish, and cities grow. It helped establish satellite remote sensing as an everyday scientific tool rather than a futuristic experiment.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: An Earth-observing satellite fits with a reflective, planetary-themed episode that encourages slowing down to notice the world.

published.fact_text: In 1616, the Catholic Church quietly added Nicolaus Copernicus’s book "On the Revolutions of the Heavenly Spheres" to its Index of Forbidden Books—73 years after it had been published. The text that had moved Earth from the center of the universe to just another planet was officially declared too dangerous for ordinary readers.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: A groundbreaking scientific book being censored speaks to the tension between curiosity and control, mirroring an episode about learning, struggle, and our culture’s view of mistakes.

published.fact_text: On March 5, 1496, England’s Henry VII granted John Cabot letters patent to "seek out, discover and find" unknown lands across the ocean. This royal paperwork quietly opened the way for one of the earliest European voyages to North America, predating more famous expeditions.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A charter to explore unknown seas resonates with an episode literally set on a sailing boat, centered on journeys over water and discovery.

published.fact_text: In 1825, Roberto Cofresí, sometimes called the "Robin Hood of the Caribbean," was finally captured after years of raiding ships around Puerto Rico. Legend says he shared plunder with the poor, blurring the line between feared pirate and folk hero.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A Caribbean pirate’s roaming life anchors well to a travel-themed, globally influenced playlist that drifts between coasts and cultures.

published.fact_text: On March 5, 1868, Arrigo Boito’s opera "Mefistofele" premiered at La Scala, offering a daring, philosophical take on Goethe’s Faust. Its initial failure—booed and cut short by censors—forced Boito to radically rewrite the work, which later returned to the stage as a respected, haunting masterpiece.

episode.title: Sedna FM #04 - Queen of Spades
episode.description: Tuning in from the planet of irrational love. Exploring love's most vulnerable side — the kind that awakens the child within us. A diverse music selection ranging from electric blues to downtempo, soul, and jazz. A tribute to the feeling that has moved artists to create the most beautiful works. Love makes us vulnerable, and there is no art without vulnerability.
songs: Alabama Shakes - Gimme All Your Love
Queens of the stone age - Make it wit chu
Etta James - I'd Rather Go Blind
Eric Clapton - Have you ever loved a woman

published.match_reason: An opera about a pact with the devil and intense human longing pairs with an episode diving into love, vulnerability, and dramatic emotion in music.

published.fact_text: On March 5, 1811, at the Battle of Barrosa during the Peninsular War, a smaller Anglo-Spanish-Portuguese force unexpectedly routed Marshal Victor’s French army. The chaotic, fiercely contested fight became a rare Allied offensive success while the much larger Siege of Cádiz still dragged on nearby.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A fierce, unlikely battlefield victory aligns with a politically charged, high-intensity episode about resistance and confrontation.

published.fact_text: In 363, the Roman emperor Julian marched out of Antioch at the head of roughly 90,000 troops to invade the Sasanian Empire. His grand campaign, driven partly by a desire to revive Rome’s old pagan identity, would end in his own death just months later in Mesopotamia.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: A reflective journey through landscapes and identity pairs with Julian’s fateful march from Antioch, another trek shaped by belief, ambition, and terrain.

published.fact_text: On March 5, 1942, Japanese forces occupied Batavia, the capital of the Dutch East Indies, after Allied troops withdrew to the interior. The city—now Jakarta—became a key hub in Japan’s wartime resource network and a stage for the region’s shifting colonial power.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A story of contested ports and shifting empires meets an episode about coastal cultures and seas as crossing points between worlds.

published.fact_text: On March 5, 1943, a general strike erupted in Athens as workers protested rumors they would be deported to Germany for forced labor. The demonstrations were so intense that the occupation authorities backed down and withdrew the mobilization decree the very next day.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

published.match_reason: Workers resisting forced deportation reflects a collective refusal to be treated as objects, echoing an episode themed around breaking boxes and reclaiming identity.

published.fact_text: On March 5, 1940, Stalin and five other Soviet politburo members signed the order that led to the execution of about 25,700 Poles, including 14,700 prisoners of war, in what became known as the Katyn massacre. For decades, the Soviet Union denied responsibility, blaming Nazi Germany until officially acknowledging the truth in 1990.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A dark episode of state control and falsified narratives is paired with a reflective show on systems that mediate truth and connection, inviting listeners to question how they receive information.

published.fact_text: On March 5, 1963, country music stars Patsy Cline, Hawkshaw Hawkins, and Cowboy Copas died together when their small plane crashed near Camden, Tennessee. The tragedy not only cut short three careers at once, it left a permanent ghost note in the history of country music.

episode.title: Morning Drops #01 - Timeless Legacy
episode.description: Gaia guides us through timeless music to start the morning with the right foot! The first episode of the Morning Drops series — classic sounds that never fade.
songs: Roberta Flack - Killing me softly with his song

published.match_reason: The loss of iconic musicians whose songs outlived them harmonizes with an episode celebrating music as a "timeless legacy."

published.fact_text: On March 5, 1966, BOAC Flight 911, a Boeing 707, broke apart in clear skies near Mount Fuji after encountering invisible, violent clear-air turbulence. The disaster highlighted that even in seemingly perfect weather, the atmosphere can hide powerful, chaotic currents.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A sudden, invisible atmospheric force that tears a plane apart mirrors the idea of an unexpected "shock" of energy, reflected in the episode’s high-voltage rock focus.

published.fact_text: On March 5, 1967, Lake Central Airlines Flight 527 crashed near Marseilles, Ohio, after a propeller blade snapped off in flight and tore into the fuselage. The failure led to changes in how propeller fatigue was monitored, making skies safer for later travelers.

episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
